title: Miami vs Purdue — Round of 32 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Round of 32 • St. Louis, MO
venue: Enterprise Center, St. Louis, Missouri • CBS
extraInfo: Winner advances to the Sweet 16 in San Jose

stakes.callout: The team that engineered the largest turnaround in Division I basketball (7-24 to 26-8) meets the team that started the season ranked No. 1 in America, stumbled to 7th in its own conference, then won the Big Ten Tournament as a 7-seed. Miami pounds the paint and dominates the glass. Purdue runs the No. 2 offense in the country through the man who just became the NCAA’s all-time career assists leader. Something’s got to give — and a Sweet 16 trip to San Jose is on the line.

cards.label: For Miami
cards.value: Eighteen months after the Final Four, the program cratered to 7-24. Then Jai Lucas rebuilt the entire roster in one offseason and went 26-8. They just won in a hostile St. Louis environment, outrebounding Missouri 46-30. A Sweet 16 berth would validate the most dramatic turnaround in college basketball — and prove this isn’t a one-weekend wonder. Three senior transfers (Reneau, Donaldson, Udeh) have extensive NCAA Tournament experience at Indiana, Auburn, Michigan, Kansas, and TCU.

cards.label: For Purdue
cards.value: Matt Painter is one win away from his 500th at Purdue. Braden Smith just broke Bobby Hurley’s all-time NCAA assists record. This team was the preseason No. 1 in America, stumbled late in the regular season (four losses in their last six), then caught fire in the Big Ten Tournament. A Sweet 16 would prove the late-season struggles were a hiccup, not a trend. And Painter knows what it feels like to lose early — FDU as a 16-seed in 2023 still stings.

cards.label: The Style Clash
cards.value: This is brute force vs. surgical precision. Miami ranks 331st in three-point attempts — they want to bully you inside. Purdue ranks 2nd in offensive efficiency and shoots 37.9% from three. Miami’s weakness is three-point defense (opponents shoot 35.4%). Purdue’s offense is built on three-point shooting. The matchup couldn’t be more direct.

cards.label: The Transfer Portal Game
cards.value: Miami was built through the portal — Reneau (Indiana), Donaldson (Auburn/Michigan), Udeh (Kansas/TCU), Washington (New Mexico). Purdue was built the old-school way, with Smith, Kaufman-Renn, and Loyer all multi-year Boilermakers. The exception: Oscar Cluff, the No. 1 transfer in the country from South Dakota State. Two philosophies. One game.

styles.title: The Monster Makeover
bullets: Paint dominance: Miami ranks 331st in three-point volume (19.1 attempts per game) because they don’t need threes. They shoot 50.1% from the field overall, with Reneau (.549), Udeh (.735), and Henderson (.563) all finishing at elite rates inside. They outscore you from the paint and the free-throw line.

Glass control: +7.7 rebounding margin (13th nationally). Against Missouri, they pulled down 16 offensive rebounds and outscored the Tigers 19-2 in second-chance points. Six players average 3.1+ rebounds. When they get extra possessions, they convert.

Donaldson runs it: Tre Donaldson averages 16.5 PPG and 5.8 APG, plays 85%+ of minutes, and has proven clutch at three different schools. He scored Miami’s final 15 points in a 67-66 win over Virginia Tech. Against Missouri: 17 points, 8 rebounds, 6 assists, 4 steals.

The "Monster" culture is real: Before game one, Jai Lucas showed a Friday the 13th clip and asked: "What kind of monster do you want to be?" After wins, a player wears a hockey mask. The identity stuck: this is the most physical team in the ACC.

Weakness: Opponents shoot 35.4% from three against Miami — and in 6 of 8 losses, opponents shot 42%+ from deep. Purdue shoots 37.9% from three as a team. That’s the direct vulnerability. Free-throw shooting (Udeh .512, Henderson .588) is also a concern. And only an 8-man rotation with minimal bench contribution means fatigue could be a factor.

styles.title: The No. 2 Offense in America
bullets: Elite efficiency: Purdue ranks 2nd in KenPom offensive efficiency (125.5) and shoots 49.9% from the field. Braden Smith’s 9.0 APG — now the NCAA’s all-time career assists leader — runs an offense that finds the right shot every time. Against Queens, they shot 63% from the field and 58% from three.

Inside-out attack: Kaufman-Renn (14.0 PPG, 57.9% FG) and Cluff (10.6 PPG, 69.8% FG) dominate inside. Loyer (13.8 PPG, 42.3% from three) stretches the defense. Smith finds the open man. When the inside game works, the threes open up. When the threes fall, the paint opens up. It’s a devastating loop.

Big Ten Tournament momentum: After losing four of their last six regular season games, Purdue ripped off four wins in four days as the 7th seed to win the Big Ten Tournament — the first 7-seed ever to do so. Beat regular-season champion Michigan 80-72 in the final. Then demolished Queens 104-71. They’re peaking.

Smith makes history: Against Queens, Smith recorded his 1,077th career assist — breaking Bobby Hurley’s 33-year-old NCAA record. He also holds the Big Ten single-season, tournament single-game (16), and tournament career (96) assist records. He’s playing the best basketball of his life at the right time.

Weakness: Defense ranks 188th in KenPom. They lost to a 16-seed (FDU) three years ago and to double-digit seeds three straight years (2021-2023). Miami’s physical rebounding and paint dominance could exploit the defense. And the late-season regular-season collapse (preseason No. 1 to 7th in the Big Ten) showed this team can lose its way.

keys.title: Purdue’s three-point shooting vs. Miami’s three-point defense.
keys.description: This is the game. Purdue shoots 37.9% from three as a team, led by Loyer at 42.3%. Miami opponents shoot 35.4% from three — and in 6 of 8 Hurricane losses, opponents shot 42%+ from deep. If Loyer and Smith are hitting from the perimeter, Miami’s pack-the-paint defense collapses because they have to extend. If Purdue’s shooters are cold, Miami can load up inside and make this a street fight in the paint — exactly the game they want.

keys.title: Miami’s rebounding vs. Purdue’s offensive efficiency.
keys.description: Miami’s +7.7 rebounding margin is 13th nationally. They grabbed 16 offensive rebounds against Missouri and outscored them 19-2 in second-chance points. But Purdue rarely gives you the chance — they shoot 49.9% from the field. When shots go in, there are no rebounds to grab. Miami needs to force misses to unleash their rebounding advantage, and Purdue’s offensive efficiency makes that incredibly hard.

keys.title: Braden Smith vs. Miami’s assist defense.
keys.description: Smith averages 9.0 APG against a Miami defense that ranks 217th in assist defense, allowing 13.9 assists per game (and 16.3 per game over their last three). This is a mismatch. Smith dissects defenses with his vision, and Miami hasn’t faced a point guard this caliber since — well, maybe ever this season. If Smith gets into the paint and starts finding Kaufman-Renn and Loyer, Miami’s defense could unravel.

keys.title: The bench depth gap.
keys.description: Miami runs an 8-man rotation with almost zero bench contribution — only one reserve logged more than four minutes against Missouri. Purdue is deeper, with Cox (8.3 PPG), Jacobsen, and Gicarri Harris providing real minutes off the bench. In a Sunday afternoon game after both teams played Friday night, fatigue matters. If this game goes to overtime or stays physical throughout, Miami’s thin rotation becomes a serious liability.

keys.title: Can Miami’s tournament veterans outperform Purdue’s system?
keys.description: Miami’s three senior transfers — Reneau (Indiana), Donaldson (Auburn, Michigan), Udeh (Kansas, TCU) — have combined for 10+ NCAA Tournament games across five different programs. Donaldson helped Michigan reach the 2025 Sweet 16. They know the moment. But Purdue’s core four (Smith, Kaufman-Renn, Loyer, Cox) have played together for years. System chemistry vs. individual experience. In March, sometimes the experienced closer (Donaldson) outweighs the system. Sometimes the system wins.

prediction.summary: This is a style clash that favors Purdue on paper but gives Miami a path. Purdue's No. 2 offense in America (125.5 efficiency) attacks Miami's biggest weakness: three-point defense (opponents shooting 35.4%, and 42%+ in 6 of 8 losses). Braden Smith's 9.0 APG against Miami's 217th-ranked assist defense is a mismatch. But Miami's physicality and rebounding (+7.7 margin) can disrupt any offense, and they proved against Missouri that they can win in a hostile environment. The key: if Loyer hits early threes, Miami has to extend their defense, and Purdue's inside-out attack opens up. If Loyer is cold, Miami can pack the paint, and Purdue's 188th-ranked defense suddenly has to stop Reneau, Udeh, and Henderson in the post. Purdue's depth advantage and three-point shooting should ultimately win out, but Donaldson's proven clutch gene makes a late-game comeback always possible.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Miami comes out physical, establishing their identity from the opening possession. Udeh contests a Kaufman-Renn post-up and Reneau draws an early foul. But Smith finds Loyer for an open three on the very first possession, and Purdue's spacing immediately poses problems. Miami answers through Reneau inside. The pace is faster than Miami wants but slower than Purdue prefers. It's 18-16 Purdue as both teams feel each other out.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Purdue's offense finds its rhythm. Smith starts seeing passing lanes that Miami's defense can't close, feeding Kaufman-Renn for a pair of easy finishes inside. Loyer hits another three, and suddenly Miami has to extend their defense. Reneau keeps the Hurricanes in it with 12 first-half points, bullying his way to the rim. But Miami's bench offers nothing — Lucas rides his starters all 20 minutes. Purdue leads 38-33 at the break, shooting 52% to Miami's 45%.

phases.title: Second Half — Miami Punches Back
phases.description: Miami opens the second half with a 7-0 run. Donaldson attacks the rim for an and-one, Henderson powers through for a layup, and Udeh grabs two offensive rebounds on the same possession. The crowd noise tilts toward the underdog. Tied at 45 with 14 minutes left. Purdue's defense — ranked 188th for a reason — struggles to contain Miami's physicality. But Smith answers with a pull-up jumper and feeds Cluff for a dunk. The teams trade blows. It's 58-55 Purdue with 8 minutes left.

phases.title: Final 8 Minutes
phases.description: This is where Purdue's depth and shooting separate them. Cox hits a corner three off a Smith assist. Loyer drills another from the wing. Miami's starters are logging heavy minutes and the legs start to go — Henderson misses two free throws (.588 FT%), and Udeh goes 0-for-2 at the line (.512 FT%). Donaldson tries to take over, scoring 8 points in the final stretch including a tough pull-up, but Smith matches him with a driving layup and a no-look feed to Kaufman-Renn. Purdue's system holds. The Boilermakers pull away in the final three minutes as Miami's shots start falling short from fatigue. Painter gets his 500th win.

projectedScore.rationale: Purdue's three-point shooting (37.9% as a team, Loyer at 42.3%) exploits Miami's biggest weakness. Smith's 9.0 APG against the 217th-ranked assist defense creates too many easy looks. Miami's rebounding keeps it close through three quarters, but their thin bench and free-throw struggles (Udeh .512, Henderson .588) cost them down the stretch. Donaldson scores 20+ but can't do it alone against Purdue's depth.

players.name: Malik Reneau
players.meta: F • 6-9, 233 lbs • Senior • All-ACC First Team
players.bio:
From Miami, Florida. Won back-to-back national titles at Montverde Academy. Former top-30 recruit who spent three seasons at Indiana before entering the portal and coming home. Three career all-conference honors across two schools. Scored 24 points (19 in the second half) in the first-round win over Missouri, including back-to-back buckets during the game-breaking 11-0 run. The interior engine of everything Lucas runs — and a Miami kid playing for his hometown school.
Read more →

players.name: Tre Donaldson
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Senior • All-ACC Second Team • 3rd School
players.bio:
Originally recruited as a football prospect but chose basketball at Auburn. Won the 2024 SEC Tournament title. Helped Michigan reach the 2025 Sweet 16. Transferred to Miami for his final year. Career-high 32 points vs. Virginia Tech — scoring all of Miami’s final 15 in a 67-66 win. Against Missouri: 17 points, 8 rebounds, 6 assists, 4 steals in a hostile St. Louis environment. A journeyman who found his peak at his third school. Plays 85%+ of Miami’s minutes. The closer.
Read more →

players.name: Ernest Udeh Jr.
players.meta: C • 6-11, 250 lbs • Senior • ACC All-Defensive Team
players.bio:
From Orlando, Florida. McDonald’s All-American. Began at Kansas, where he set the school record with 21 consecutive made field goals. Started all 59 games over two seasons at TCU. Now anchoring Miami’s defense with 9.3 RPG (2nd in ACC) and 1.4 BPG. Grabbed 10 rebounds (5 offensive) against Missouri, anchoring Miami’s 46-30 rebounding edge. His .735 FG% means he only takes shots he can make. The Achilles’ heel: .512 free-throw shooting.
Read more →

players.name: Shelton Henderson
players.meta: F • 6-6, 240 lbs • Freshman • Five-Star, Houston POY
players.bio:
From Bellaire, Texas. 2025 Houston High School Player of the Year. Five-star recruit rated No. 23 overall. Was Jai Lucas’s first five-star recruit at Miami — Lucas recruited him while still at Duke. At 240 pounds as a freshman, he’s one of the most physically imposing first-years in the country. Scored 15 points with 6 rebounds against Missouri. NBA draft prospect. His three-point shot (.271) and free throws (.588) need work, but his physicality fits the "Monster" identity perfectly.
Read more →

players.name: Braden Smith
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Senior • Consensus All-American • NCAA Assists Leader
players.bio:
From Westfield, Indiana. Both parents played college basketball at Arkansas Tech. High school all-time leader in points (1,629) and assists (453) at Westfield, leading them to their first sectional title in 105 years. Just became the NCAA Division I all-time career assists leader (1,077+), breaking Bobby Hurley’s 33-year-old record during the Queens game. Also holds the Big Ten single-season and tournament assist records. Scored 26 points (10-15 FG) with 8 assists against Queens. His 9.0 APG runs the No. 2 offense in America. He sees passes nobody else sees.
Read more →

players.name: Trey Kaufman-Renn
players.bio:
From Sellersburg, Indiana. Four-star recruit who chose Purdue over Indiana, North Carolina, and Virginia. Majoring in Philosophy. The interior anchor who makes Purdue’s offense elite with 57.9% shooting. Scored 25 points with 9 rebounds against Queens, exploiting the frontcourt mismatch all night. His scoring dropped from 20.1 PPG last season, which was part of Purdue’s mid-season struggles, but he’s peaking at the right time.
Read more →

players.name: Fletcher Loyer
players.bio:
From Fort Wayne, Indiana. His father John is a scout for the LA Clippers and was interim coach of the Detroit Pistons. His grandfather played at Purdue in 1964. His brother Foster played at Michigan State. Won the national High School 3-Point Championship. Shoots 42.3% from three — the perimeter weapon that opens up Smith’s passing lanes and Kaufman-Renn’s interior game. When Loyer is hitting, Purdue’s offense is virtually unguardable. He directly attacks Miami’s biggest weakness: three-point defense.
Read more →

players.name: Oscar Cluff
players.meta: F • 6-11, 250 lbs • Senior • No. 1 Transfer in America
players.bio:
From Sunshine Coast, Australia. Ranked as the No. 1 transfer in the country when he committed to Purdue. Dominated at South Dakota State (17.6 PPG, 12.3 RPG, 63.4% FG) — one of only 10 players in NCAA history to match that stat line, alongside Zach Edey, Blake Griffin, and Tim Duncan. Against Queens: 9 points, 11 rebounds, 5 assists, 4 blocks. Shoots 69.8% from the field — among the best in the country.
Read more →

funFacts.title: The All-Time Assists King (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Braden Smith broke Bobby Hurley’s 33-year-old NCAA career assists record during the Queens game, recording his 1,077th assist just eight minutes in. He also holds the Big Ten single-season record, the Big Ten Tournament single-game record (16), and the tournament career record (96). He’s the most prolific passer in college basketball history.

funFacts.title: The Friday the 13th Speech (Miami)
funFacts.body: Before Miami’s first game, Jai Lucas showed his team the scene from the 2009 Friday the 13th reboot where Jason Voorhees discovers his hockey mask. He asked: "What kind of monster do you want to be?" After each win, a player who embodies team values wears a hockey mask ceremonially. The "Monster" identity stuck.

funFacts.title: Cradle of Astronauts (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Purdue has produced 24 astronauts, including Neil Armstrong (first person on the Moon) and Gene Cernan (last person on the Moon). It’s known as the "Cradle of Astronauts." Other notable Purdue alumni: Drew Brees and Orville Redenbacher.

funFacts.title: The Largest Turnaround in D-I (Miami)
funFacts.body: Miami went from 7-24 to 26-8 in one season — a 19-win improvement that’s the largest single-season turnaround in Division I basketball. Jim Larrañaga retired mid-season. Jai Lucas rebuilt the entire roster through the transfer portal in a single offseason. And now they’re in the Round of 32.

funFacts.title: The FDU Nightmare (Purdue)
funFacts.body: In 2023, No. 16 Fairleigh Dickinson upset No. 1 Purdue 63-58. It was only the second 16-over-1 upset ever. Before that, Purdue lost to 13-seed North Texas (2021) and 15-seed Saint Peter’s (2022). Three straight years of double-digit seed losses. The scar tissue is real.

funFacts.title: The Transfer Portal Game (Both)
funFacts.body: Miami was built through the portal: Reneau (Indiana), Donaldson (Auburn/Michigan), Udeh (Kansas/TCU), Washington (New Mexico). Purdue was built the old-school way, with Smith, Kaufman-Renn, and Loyer as multi-year Boilermakers. The one exception: Oscar Cluff, the No. 1 transfer in America. Two philosophies collide.

funFacts.title: Football Prospect Turned Hoops Star (Miami)
funFacts.body: Tre Donaldson was originally recruited as a football prospect before choosing basketball at Auburn. He won the 2024 SEC Tournament title, helped Michigan reach the 2025 Sweet 16, and now leads Miami with 16.5 PPG and 5.8 APG at his third school.

funFacts.title: The Boilermaker Special (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Purdue’s mascot is a locomotive mounted on a car chassis — claimed to be the world’s largest, fastest, heaviest, and loudest collegiate mascot. First built in 1940, now on its seventh version, it travels to every away football game at speeds up to 75 MPH. The current model still carries the bell and whistle from the original.

funFacts.title: Why an Ibis? (Miami)
funFacts.body: Sebastian the Ibis was chosen because the white ibis is the last wildlife to take shelter before a hurricane and the first to reappear afterward — symbolizing bravery and resilience. Originally named "Icky," the mascot was later renamed after San Sebastian Hall.

funFacts.title: Philosophy Major Dominates the Paint (Purdue)
funFacts.body: Trey Kaufman-Renn is majoring in Philosophy at Purdue while shooting 57.9% from the field. He scored 25 points and grabbed 9 rebounds against Queens. As Descartes might say: "I think, therefore I dunk."

funFacts.title: The Rock Went to "The U" (Miami)
funFacts.body: Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson graduated from the University of Miami in 1995 with a degree in criminology and physiology. Other famous alumni: Gloria Estefan, Ray Liotta, and Sylvester Stallone (who attended but didn’t graduate).

funFacts.title: Painter’s 500th Win (Purdue)
funFacts.body: A Purdue victory would be Matt Painter’s 500th career win at Purdue. He’s been the head coach for 21 years, played at Purdue from 1989-93, and has been named Big Ten Coach of the Year five times. He also won the 2025 Wooden "Legends of Coaching" Award.

history.callout: Purdue leads the all-time series 3-1, though these programs have rarely crossed paths. They’ve never met in the NCAA Tournament. Purdue has 38 tournament appearances and 3 Final Fours but has never won a national championship. Miami reached the Final Four in 2023 under Jim Larrañaga, then cratered to 7-24, and now is back in the tournament under a brand-new coach. Both programs are chasing elusive breakthroughs — Purdue wants its first title, Miami wants to prove its resurrection is real.

subsections.title: Purdue’s Championship Chase — Always the Bridesmaid

timeline.text: Led by All-American Rick Mount, Purdue reaches the National Championship Game. Loses to UCLA and John Wooden. The closest they’ve come.
Joe Barry Carroll leads Purdue to the Final Four. UCLA ends the run again in the semifinals.
The nightmare. No. 16 Fairleigh Dickinson upsets No. 1 Purdue 63-58. Only the second 16-over-1 upset in history. The worst loss in program history.
Redemption run. Zach Edey leads Purdue to the National Championship Game for the first time in 55 years. They lose to UConn 75-60. So close, again.

The senior class of Smith, Kaufman-Renn, and Loyer — together for four years — makes one final push. After falling from preseason No. 1 to 7th in the Big Ten, they won the conference tournament and demolished Queens 104-71. The championship chase continues.

subsections.title: Miami’s Rise, Fall, and Resurrection
subsections.content:
In April 2023, Jim Larrañaga led Miami to the Final Four as a 5-seed — beating top-seeded Houston and 2-seed Texas. It was the program’s greatest achievement. Then eight players entered the portal. The 2024-25 season was a disaster: 7-24. Larrañaga retired mid-season at 75.

Enter Jai Lucas, a 37-year-old first-time head coach. He rebuilt the entire roster in a single offseason — Reneau from Indiana, Donaldson from Michigan, Udeh from TCU, Henderson as a five-star freshman. The result: 26-8 and back in the NCAA Tournament. They just beat Missouri 80-66 in a hostile environment, outrebounding them by 16. The resurrection is real, and now they face the No. 2 seed.

subsections.title: The 2-vs-7 Matchup: Purdue’s Underdog History
subsections.content:
Historically, the 2-seed wins the 2-vs-7 matchup about 71% of the time. But Purdue has a complicated March history. They lost to a 16-seed (FDU in 2023), a 15-seed (Saint Peter’s in 2022), and a 13-seed (North Texas in 2021). Three straight years of double-digit seed losses.

Miami isn’t a typical 7-seed, either. They’re KenPom 31st with a 33rd-ranked offense and 38th-ranked defense. They have three senior transfers with 10+ combined NCAA Tournament games across Indiana, Auburn, Michigan, Kansas, and TCU. They’re battle-tested in a way most 7-seeds aren’t. This is a genuine threat to Purdue’s Sweet 16 hopes.
